quiz_questions.question_text: What is centripetal acceleration?

options.text: Acceleration that acts in the direction of motion along the tangent, causing the object to speed up or slow down as it travels along a curved path
Zero acceleration during circular motion, since objects moving at constant speed in a circle have no change in velocity and therefore no net acceleration
Acceleration directed away from the center of a circular path, pushing the object outward and increasing the radius of the curved trajectory over time
Acceleration directed toward the center of a circular path that causes an object to follow a curved trajectory

quiz_questions.explanation: Centripetal acceleration always points toward the center of the circle, continuously changing the direction of velocity to produce circular motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for centripetal acceleration?

options.text: a_c = r divided by v squared, where r is the radius of the circular path and v is the linear speed of the object moving along the circumference
a_c = v times r, where the centripetal acceleration equals the product of the linear speed and the radius of the circular path being followed
a_c = v divided by r, where the centripetal acceleration equals the linear speed divided by the radius of the circular path without squaring velocity
a_c = v²/r, where v is the speed and r is the radius

quiz_questions.explanation: Centripetal acceleration = v²/r. A higher speed or smaller radius produces greater centripetal acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What provides the centripetal force for a car turning on a flat road?

options.text: Air resistance pushing inward on the car body as it moves through the turn
The engine force propelling the car forward along its direction of travel
Gravity pulling the car downward toward the center of the Earth's surface
Friction between the tires and the road

quiz_questions.explanation: On a flat road, static friction between the tires and road provides the inward (centripetal) force needed to change the car's direction during a turn.

quiz_questions.question_text: What provides the centripetal force for the Moon orbiting Earth?

options.text: Gravitational force between Earth and the Moon
The Moon's own propulsion
Friction with space
The Sun's gravity only

quiz_questions.explanation: Gravity provides the centripetal force that keeps the Moon in orbit around Earth. The gravitational pull toward Earth continuously deflects the Moon's path into a circle.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to an object moving in a circle if the centripetal force suddenly disappears?

options.text: It stops immediately at the point of release, because without a centripetal force the object has no mechanism to continue any form of motion at all
It continues moving in the same circular path, because the inertia of circular motion maintains the curved trajectory even after the centripetal force vanishes
It moves radially outward from the center of the circle, accelerating away from the center point due to the residual centrifugal effect from the prior rotation
It moves in a straight line tangent to the circle at the point of release

quiz_questions.explanation: By Newton's first law, without net force the object continues in a straight line. This line is tangent to the circle at the release point.

quiz_questions.question_text: An object moves in a circle at constant speed. Is it accelerating?

options.text: No, constant speed means there is no acceleration present, because acceleration requires a change in the numerical value of speed not just a direction change
Only if the radius of the circular path changes during the motion, since constant radius combined with constant speed means zero acceleration at all times
Only if there is friction acting on the object, because without a friction force there is no mechanism to produce acceleration during uniform circular motion
Yes — the direction of velocity is continuously changing, which constitutes acceleration even though speed is constant

quiz_questions.explanation: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity (a vector). In uniform circular motion, the direction changes continuously, so there is always a centripetal acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for centripetal force?

options.text: F_c = mvr
F_c = mv²/r
F_c = mv/r
F_c = mr/v²

quiz_questions.explanation: F_c = ma_c = m(v²/r) = mv²/r. The centripetal force is the net force directed toward the center.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is centripetal force a new type of force?

options.text: Yes it is a completely new type of force that exists only during circular motion and does not correspond to any known fundamental physical interaction between objects
No — centripetal force is the name for whatever force(s) point toward the center: gravity, friction, tension, etc.
No it is the same as centrifugal force, since centripetal and centrifugal are just two different names for the identical outward-pointing force in circular motion
Yes it is a fundamental force of nature alongside gravity, electromagnetism, and the nuclear forces, acting specifically on objects that follow curved trajectories

quiz_questions.explanation: Centripetal force is not a new force — it is a role played by real forces (gravity, friction, tension, normal force) that happen to point toward the center of the circular path.

quiz_questions.question_text: A ball on a string moves in a horizontal circle. What force provides the centripetal force?

options.text: The tension in the string
Air resistance
Gravity
The ball's momentum

quiz_questions.explanation: The string's tension pulls the ball toward the center of the circle, providing the centripetal force needed for circular motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: As double the speed of an object in circular motion (keeping r constant), the centripetal force:

options.text: It doubles, since the centripetal force is directly proportional to speed and doubling the speed doubles the required inward force by the same factor
It halves, because increasing the speed allows the object to maintain the circle with less centripetal force due to the greater momentum carrying it around
It stays the same, because the radius has not changed and centripetal force depends only on the radius and mass, not on the speed of the circular motion
Quadruples — F_c = mv²/r, so doubling v makes F_c four times larger

quiz_questions.explanation: F_c = mv²/r. If v → 2v: F_c = m(2v)²/r = 4mv²/r. The force quadruples because of the v² dependence.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes uniform circular motion?

options.text: Motion in a straight line at constant speed, where the velocity remains entirely unchanged in both magnitude and direction throughout the entire duration
Any curved motion of any kind, including elliptical, parabolic, and hyperbolic paths, since all curved trajectories qualify as uniform circular motion in physics
Motion along a circular path at constant speed, where the velocity direction changes continuously and the acceleration is always directed toward the center
Motion in a circle with continuously increasing speed, where the object accelerates tangentially while also experiencing a radially inward centripetal acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: In uniform circular motion, speed is constant but velocity changes direction continuously. The acceleration (a = v²/r) and net force always point toward the center.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes centripetal acceleration?

options.text: Acceleration directed tangent to the circular path at the point of interest, which causes the object to change its speed as it moves along the curved trajectory
Acceleration that increases the speed of an object moving in a circle, acting along the direction of motion to continuously add kinetic energy to the system
Acceleration directed radially outward from the center of the circle, pushing the object farther away from the center and increasing the radius of the path
The acceleration directed radially inward (toward the center of the circle) experienced by any object moving along a curved path, with magnitude v²/r

quiz_questions.explanation: Centripetal means 'center-seeking.' The acceleration has magnitude v²/r (or ω²r) and always points toward the center of curvature.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes centripetal force?

options.text: The force that speeds up objects moving in a circle, acting along the direction of motion tangentially and increasing the kinetic energy of the rotating object
A new fundamental force unique to circular motion that does not correspond to any other known force, existing as its own independent category in the force classification
A force that acts outward from the center of the circular path, pushing the object away from the center and opposing the inward centripetal acceleration at all times
The net force directed toward the center of a circular path, responsible for continuously changing the direction of an object's velocity — equal to mv²/r

quiz_questions.explanation: Centripetal force is the net inward force that causes circular motion. It is provided by real forces (gravity, tension, friction, etc.) directed toward the center.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes centrifugal force?

options.text: A real physical force that pushes objects outward during circular motion, produced by the rotation itself and acting through direct physical contact with the object
Exactly the same force as centripetal force described using a different name, since both terms refer to the identical inward-directed force in circular motion problems
The Newton's third law reaction partner to the centripetal force, acting on the same object in the opposite direction and partially canceling the inward acceleration
A fictitious (pseudo) force that appears to push objects outward in a rotating reference frame — it has no physical agent and exists only in non-inertial frames

quiz_questions.explanation: Centrifugal force is not a real force — it is an apparent force experienced in rotating frames. In an inertial frame, objects move in straight lines; the 'outward push' is simply inertia.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes angular velocity?

options.text: The rate of change of angular acceleration with respect to time, representing how quickly the angular acceleration itself is changing during rotational motion
The linear speed of an object moving in a circle, measured in meters per second along the tangent to the circular path at the point of interest on the circumference
The rate at which an object rotates or revolves, measured in radians per second (ω = 2π/T = 2πf)
The centripetal acceleration expressed in angular units, which gives the same information as a_c = v squared over r but measured using radians instead of meters

quiz_questions.explanation: Angular velocity ω tells how fast the angle changes. It relates to linear speed by v = ωr and to period by ω = 2π/T.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the period of circular motion?

options.text: The speed divided by the radius
The number of revolutions per second
The time T for one complete revolution around the circular path
The distance around the circle

quiz_questions.explanation: Period T is the time for one full cycle. It relates to speed: T = 2πr/v, and to angular velocity: T = 2π/ω.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes frequency in circular motion?

options.text: The linear speed of the object measured in meters per second as it travels along the circumference of the circular path at any given instant during the motion
The radius of the circular path measured in meters, representing the perpendicular distance from the center of the circle to the object moving along the circumference
Exactly the same quantity as angular velocity, since frequency and angular velocity are two interchangeable names for the identical rotational measurement in physics
The number of complete revolutions per unit time, f = 1/T, measured in hertz (Hz)

quiz_questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = ω/(2π). It counts how many full circles per second. 1 Hz = 1 revolution per second.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes tangential velocity?

options.text: The component of velocity directed along the tangent to the circular path — its magnitude is the speed v = ωr, and its direction is continuously changing
Velocity directed toward the center of the circular path, pointing radially inward and responsible for maintaining the constant radius of the circular trajectory
The rate at which the speed of the object changes over time, representing the tangential acceleration that speeds up or slows down the object along its curved path
Velocity directed away from the center of the circular path, pointing radially outward and responsible for the apparent centrifugal effect observed in rotating frames

quiz_questions.explanation: Tangential velocity is perpendicular to the radius at every point. Its magnitude is constant in uniform circular motion, but its direction changes continuously.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a banked curve?

options.text: A curve on a perfectly flat and level road surface, where all centripetal force must be supplied entirely by friction between the tires and the road surface
A curve with a special extra-grip friction coating applied to the road surface, designed to increase the maximum centripetal force available for turning vehicles
A curved road or track tilted at an angle so that the normal force provides a component of centripetal force, reducing reliance on friction
A curve designed to be safe only at very slow speeds below 20 km/h, because the banking angle becomes dangerous and destabilizing at higher velocities

quiz_questions.explanation: On a banked curve, the road is tilted so N sinθ contributes centripetal force. At the ideal banking angle, no friction is needed: tanθ = v²/(rg).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes non-uniform circular motion?

options.text: Circular motion at constant speed where only centripetal acceleration exists, and no tangential acceleration component is present at any point along the path
Motion that follows a straight line rather than a circular arc, because the net force is zero and there is no centripetal acceleration to curve the trajectory
Motion that follows an elliptical orbit rather than a perfect circle, because real orbits always have some eccentricity due to perturbations from nearby objects
Circular motion where the speed changes — the object has both centripetal acceleration (toward center) and tangential acceleration (along the path)

quiz_questions.explanation: In non-uniform circular motion, the speed changes, producing tangential acceleration. The total acceleration is the vector sum of centripetal and tangential components.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car moves around a circular track of radius 50 m at 20 m/s. What is its centripetal acceleration?

options.text: 1000 m/s squared — using a_c = v times r = 20 times 50 = 1000 m/s squared, because centripetal acceleration equals the product of velocity and radius
8 m/s² — a_c = v²/r = 400/50 = 8 m/s² toward the center
0.4 m/s squared — using a_c = v divided by r = 20/50 = 0.4 m/s squared, because centripetal acceleration equals velocity divided by radius without squaring
2.5 m/s squared — using a_c = r divided by v squared = 50/400 = 0.125 rounded to approximately 2.5 m/s squared by applying a correction factor for the radius

quiz_questions.explanation: a_c = v²/r = 20²/50 = 400/50 = 8 m/s² directed toward the center of the track.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 0.5 kg ball on a 1 m string moves in a horizontal circle at 4 m/s. What is the tension in the string?

options.text: 2 N — using T = mv/r = 0.5 times 4 / 1 = 2 N, because tension equals mass times velocity divided by the radius of the circular path without squaring speed
32 N — using T = mv squared times r = 0.5 times 16 times 2 = 16 then doubled to 32 N, because the formula multiplies by radius rather than dividing by it
4 N — using T = mv = 0.5 times 4 = 2 then doubled for the circular motion factor giving approximately 4 N, because centripetal force equals momentum divided by time

quiz_questions.explanation: T = mv²/r = 0.5 × 4²/1 = 0.5 × 16 = 8 N. Tension provides the centripetal force.

quiz_questions.question_text: A satellite orbits Earth at radius r with speed v. If it moves to an orbit with radius 2r, how does the required centripetal acceleration change?

options.text: It doubles, because centripetal acceleration is directly proportional to the orbital radius, so doubling the radius doubles the required centripetal acceleration
It becomes one-fourth — a_c = v²/r, and for a circular orbit v² = GM/r, so a_c = GM/r², which is inversely proportional to r²: doubling r gives a_c/4
It halves, because centripetal acceleration equals v squared over r, and doubling the radius alone divides the acceleration by two for the same orbital speed value
It stays the same, because the centripetal acceleration of a satellite depends only on the mass of the planet and is independent of the orbital radius or speed

quiz_questions.explanation: For a gravitational orbit: a_c = GM/r². At 2r: a_c = GM/(2r)² = GM/(4r²) = (1/4)(GM/r²). The centripetal acceleration is one-fourth.

quiz_questions.question_text: You swing a bucket of water over your head in a vertical circle. At the top of the circle, the water doesn't fall out. Why?

options.text: The bucket creates a partial vacuum above the water surface that holds the water in place, preventing it from falling due to the suction effect of the low pressure
Surface tension of the water molecules creates a strong cohesive film across the bucket opening that prevents any water from spilling even when the bucket inverts
Centrifugal force pushes the water into the bucket from the inside, because centrifugal force is a real outward force that acts on objects during circular motion

At the top, both gravity and the normal force from the bucket provide centripetal force. The water doesn't fall because the required centripetal acceleration (v²/r) exceeds or equals g, keeping the water pressed into the bucket

quiz_questions.explanation: At the top: mg + N = mv²/r. If v²/r ≥ g, then N ≥ 0 (bucket still pushes on water). The water needs centripetal acceleration toward center, and gravity alone isn't enough — the bucket floor provides the rest.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 1000 kg car rounds a flat curve (r = 100 m). The coefficient of static friction is 0.6. What is the maximum safe speed? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 10 m/s — using v = mu_s times g = 0.6 times 10 = 6, adjusted to approximately 10 m/s by including a radius correction factor for the flat curve geometry
6 m/s — using v = mu_s times r = 0.6 times 100/10 = 6 m/s, because maximum speed equals the friction coefficient multiplied by the radius over gravity
60 m/s — using v = mu_s times g times r = 0.6 times 10 times 100 = 600 then taking the square root and adding a factor to get approximately 60 m/s

quiz_questions.question_text: A fan blade rotates at 120 RPM. A point on the tip (r = 0.3 m) has what centripetal acceleration?

options.text: a_c = ω²r = (4π)² × 0.3 ≈ 47.4 m/s² — 120 RPM = 2 rev/s, ω = 2π × 2 = 4π rad/s
12 m/s squared — using a_c = RPM times r = 120 times 0.3 divided by 3 = 12 m/s squared, because centripetal acceleration equals RPM times the radius scaled down
360 m/s squared — using a_c = RPM times 2pi times r = 120 times 6.28 times 0.3 divided by some factor giving approximately 360 m/s squared centripetal acceleration
0.3 m/s squared — using a_c = r = 0.3 m/s squared directly, because the centripetal acceleration numerically equals the radius when angular velocity is 1 radian per second

quiz_questions.question_text: A stone on a string moves in a vertical circle with radius 0.8 m. What is the minimum speed at the top for the string to remain taut?

options.text: √(gr) = √(9.8 × 0.8) ≈ 2.8 m/s — at minimum speed, tension = 0 and gravity alone provides centripetal force: mg = mv²/r
9.8 m/s — using v = g = 9.8 m/s, because the minimum speed at the top of a vertical circle always equals the gravitational acceleration expressed in speed units
0 m/s — the minimum speed at the top is zero because the string can remain taut as long as gravity continues to act downward on the stone at every point in the circle
4.4 m/s — using v = 2 times g times r = 2 times 9.8 times 0.8 = 15.68, then taking the square root to get approximately 4.0 which rounds up to 4.4 m/s

quiz_questions.explanation: At the top, minimum tension = 0: mg = mv²/r → v² = gr → v = √(9.8 × 0.8) = √7.84 ≈ 2.8 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: An engineer designs a banked curve for a highway with r = 200 m and target speed 25 m/s. What should the banking angle be? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: θ = arctan(v²/(rg)) = arctan(625/2000) = arctan(0.3125) ≈ 17.4°
30 degrees — using theta = v divided by r = 25/200 = 0.125 then converted to degrees by multiplying by 240 to give approximately 30 degrees banking angle
45 degrees — the optimal banking angle is always 45 degrees for any speed and radius combination, since 45 degrees maximizes the centripetal force component
12.5 degrees — using theta = v divided by (r times g) = 25 / (200 times 10) = 0.0125 then converted to degrees by multiplying by 1000 to give 12.5 degrees

quiz_questions.explanation: tanθ = v²/(rg) = 625/2000 = 0.3125. θ = arctan(0.3125) ≈ 17.4°. At this angle, no friction is needed at the design speed.

quiz_questions.question_text: A child on a merry-go-round sits 2 m from the center. The ride completes one revolution in 4 seconds. What centripetal force does the 30 kg child experience?

options.text: 15 N — using F = m times r divided by T = 30 times 2 / 4 = 15 N, because centripetal force equals mass times radius divided by the period of revolution
30 N — using F = m times g divided by 10 = 30 times 10 / 10 = 30 N, because centripetal force on a merry-go-round equals the gravitational weight of the child
60 N — using F = m times v = 30 times 2 = 60 N, because the centripetal force equals the product of mass and the linear velocity at the child's position

quiz_questions.question_text: A washing machine spin cycle rotates at 1200 RPM with drum radius 0.25 m. Compare the centripetal acceleration to g.

options.text: About 1g — the centripetal acceleration in a washing machine drum is roughly equal to gravitational acceleration, producing a gentle force similar to normal weight
About 40g — the centripetal acceleration is approximately 40 times gravitational acceleration, which is typical for household appliances with fast spinning motors
a_c = ω²r = (40π)² × 0.25 ≈ 3948 m/s² ≈ 403g — the acceleration is about 400 times gravity!
About 10g — the centripetal acceleration is approximately 10 times gravitational acceleration, since most spinning drums produce a moderate centripetal force level
